title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Monthly Income Scheme
In the world of investments, when it comes to payouts, some investments offer a lump sum amount, while others have the provision of offering monthly income. If you want a regular source of income, you can consider avenues that offer the flexibility of offering monthly payouts. There are different types of schemes offering a monthly income benefit. Some offer assured returns, while some are market-linked. Let’s understand the meaning of monthly income schemes and which investment avenues fit the bill.
Understanding Monthly Income Schemes
In simple words, monthly income schemes depict investments that can offer monthly payouts. In other words, you can get a regular source of income from these avenues. This income can help in supplementing your primary source of income or become your source of income after retirement.
There are different types of monthly investment plans available in the market. Each scheme has its own risk profile, investment tenure, tax implications, and the type of return that it offers.
Features of Monthly Income Plans
Some of the salient features of monthly income plans are as follows -
Monthly Payout Options
As is evident from the name itself, these types of plans offer monthly payouts. You can either get the interest earned on your investment as monthly income or withdraw a part of your investment every month.
Investment Flexibility
Many monthly income plans allow you to invest affordably. You can invest online or offline, making the scheme flexible and easily accessible.
Customisable Payout Choices
While monthly income plans offer monthly income, in some plans, you have the choice to customise the payout frequency. For instance, non-cumulative fixed deposits allow you to choose to receive the income every quarter, half-year, or year, rather than monthly.
Given this scope of customisation, you can choose a payout option that matches your financial needs.
Monthly Income Scheme Options
Some of the common investment options offering monthly income payouts are as follows -
Post Office Monthly Income Scheme (POMIS)
The Post Office Monthly Income Scheme is a low-risk, fixed-income investment scheme. You can make a lump sum deposit, and the tenure of the scheme is 5 years. The interest rate is 7.40% per annum, which is paid monthly. The benefits of POMIS include the following -
Low Minimum Investment
You can start investing in the POMIS with as low as ₹1000 and in multiples of ₹1000. The maximum deposit amount is ₹9 lakhs for a single account and ₹15 lakhs for joint accounts.
Flexible Tenure
While the tenure of the account is 5 years, you are allowed to withdraw your deposit after one year from the date on which the account was opened. If you close the account before the completion of 3 years, 2% of the deposited amount would be deducted. If you close the account after completing 3 years but before maturity, 1% of the deposited amount would be deducted.
Fixed Deposits (FDs)
Fixed deposits (FDs) are also considered low-risk investment options as they offer fixed returns on your investments. You can deposit a lump sum amount for a specific term and get a guaranteed interest rate. Fixed deposits are offered by both banks and financial institutions, and 5-year FDs offered by banks are also allowed as a deduction under Section 80C.
The benefits of FDs are as follows -
Fixed Interest Rates
The interest rate of the FD is fixed when you open the account. Once fixed, it does not change over the investment tenure. You can enjoy fixed returns without market volatility and build a guaranteed corpus.
Options for Maturity
On maturity, you have the option to reinvest your FD for continued investment. Alternatively, you can withdraw the FD and use the proceeds to fund your needs.
Interest Payment Options
If you choose cumulative FDs, the interest income gets accumulated with the deposited amount. The total interest earned and the deposited amount are, then, paid on maturity. However, with non-cumulative FDs, you can choose to receive the interest income monthly, half-yearly, annually, or quarterly. On maturity, the deposited amount is paid back.
Security
FDs issued by banks are insured up to ₹5 lakhs by the DICGC (Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation). This minimises the credit risk of the deposit and secures your investment.
National Pension Scheme (NPS)
The National Pension System (NPS) is a retirement-oriented saving scheme which enjoys government backing. The scheme helps you invest in market-linked securities and earn market-linked returns over time. Some of the benefits of the NPS scheme are as follows -
Investment Alternatives
NPS offers you a choice of two types of accounts – Tier I and Tier II. While Tier I is mandatory, Tier II is optional. Moreover, you get a choice of two types of investment strategies – Auto Choice and Active Choice. Under the former strategy, your contributions are allocated to different asset classes in a specific strategy depending on your risk profile. However, under the Active strategy, you can choose the asset class yourself. There are different types of asset classes under NPS, like equity, corporate debt, government securities, etc. You can choose a fund that matches your risk appetite and investment preference.
Portability
If your employer is also contributing to the NPS scheme, you don’t have to worry about continuity even when you change jobs. The NPS account is completely portable across all employers, even in different geographical locations.
Retirement Benefits
On maturity of the NPS scheme, you can withdraw up to 60% of the accumulated corpus in a lump sum if you are a government employee. For non-government employees, withdrawal is allowed for up to 80% of the accumulated corpus. However, if the corpus is up to ₹8 lakhs, you can withdraw the entire amount in a lump sum. This withdrawn corpus can be used for meeting your expenses and financial goals after retirement. Moreover, the remaining corpus is used to pay annuities, which can become a retirement benefit and ensure a source of regular income in your golden years.
National Savings Certificate (NSC)
The National Savings Certificate is another investment scheme that offers fixed returns. It is available through post offices and authorised banks, and you can open an account with as little as ₹1000. The current interest rate for NSC is 7.7% per annum.
Safety
The scheme is backed by the Indian government, which eliminates any credit risk. The interest rates are fixed, and you get the deposited amount plus the interest earned on maturity.
Maturity Period
The NSC has a tenure of 5 years after which it matures. Though partial withdrawals are allowed, they might reduce your overall earnings.
Factors to Consider Before Choosing a Monthly Income Plan
With the range of monthly income plans available, you get the choice of choosing a plan that fits your needs. However, before choosing, it is important to assess the following factors –
Risk Tolerance
Assess your risk appetite, i.e., your risk-taking ability. This would help you choose the right investment plan. For instance, if you don’t mind taking risks, you can choose the NPS scheme for market-linked returns. On the other hand, if you don’t like risks and want assured returns, FDs or POMIS can be a more suitable fit.
Liquidity
Liquidity means how soon you can convert your investments into cash. Assess your liquidity needs to choose the correct avenues. If you need liquidity, choose avenues that do not have a premature withdrawal penalty that might hamper your returns. On the other hand, if liquidity is not a concern, you can choose avenues with a lock-in period.
Investment Horizon
The investment horizon means how long you can stay invested. Different investment avenues have different investment tenures. So, it is recommended to choose avenues whose tenures match your investment horizon.
Minimum Investment
Check the minimum investment required for the investment that you prefer. Ensure that your savings meet the minimum investment criteria so that you can successfully invest in the scheme.
Tax Implications
Some monthly income schemes offer tax benefits on investments and, in some cases, also on the returns. Checking the tax implications of each scheme can help you choose tax-efficient options that can help in reducing your tax liability.
Fees and Charges
Check the fees and charges associated with the different types of investment schemes so that you know how much of your savings are used for meeting the charges.
Interest Rates
Knowing the interest rates of different schemes can help you compare and find a scheme that offers the maximum possible returns.
Diversification
Diversification means choosing different types of assets in your portfolio so that you can mitigate investment risks and enhance the return potential. So, try to diversify your investments across equity, debt, and other asset classes.
Professional Advice
If you are new to investments or if you need expert assistance in selecting the right monthly income schemes, you can seek professional advice.
Who Should Choose a Monthly Income Plan?
A monthly income plan might be suitable for the following types of individuals -
Retirees and Pensioners
Individuals who have retired and earn a pension might choose monthly income plans to create a source of regular income for meeting their lifestyle expenses.
Risk-Averse Investors
If you are averse to investment risks, you can choose monthly income plans, which have the potential to generate monthly returns.
Individuals with Irregular Income
If your income is not fixed, monthly income plans can help create a regular source of income. This can help you meet your everyday expenses without straining your budget.
People Planning for Short to Medium Term Goals
If you have to save up for a short or medium-term goal, a monthly income plan can prove useful. With fixed-income plans, you can create a secure corpus and enjoy capital safety.
Senior Citizens Seeking Tax Benefits
As many monthly income plans offer tax-saving benefits, senior citizens can not only create a regular income stream but also save taxes on the same with such plans.
Key Takeaways
Monthly income plans are investment schemes that have the potential to generate monthly income inflows.
The features of monthly income plans include regular interest payouts, flexibility in investment, and customisable payout options.
Some of the common monthly income schemes include POMIS, National Pension Scheme, FDs, and NSC.
When selecting a monthly income plan, consider your risk appetite, investment duration, tax implications, minimum investment, interest offered, and other factors.
Diversify your portfolio across asset classes to minimise risks and enhance the return potential.
Monthly income plans are suitable for senior citizens, retirees and pensioners, investors having a low risk profile, investors saving for short or medium term goals, and individuals with irregular incomes.
Conclusion
Different types of monthly income schemes have different risk profiles, return potential, tax implications, and investment tenures. Understand each scheme thoroughly before investing in it. Choose a scheme if it matches your financial goals, investment period, and risk appetite. Also, try to diversify and seek professional help if needed. Monthly income plans are a good choice for creating an additional income inflow and can be considered if they fit your needs.
FAQs
Which is the Right Monthly Income Scheme (MIS)?
The right monthly income scheme depends on your financial goals, risk-taking ability, investment period, and affordability. You can compare the available options and choose a scheme that matches your financial needs.
What are the key benefits of investing in a Monthly Income Scheme?
Some of the key benefits of investing in monthly income schemes include a regular source of inflow, customised payout options, tax savings with some instruments, and easy investments.
Which Monthly Income Plans are considered low-risk?
Plans that offer fixed and guaranteed returns can be considered low-risk since they are not exposed to market volatility. Moreover, if the plans are backed by the government, they do not have any credit risk, making them safer.
How can one apply for a Monthly Income Scheme?
You can apply for a monthly income scheme through the issuer. Choose the scheme, fill out the investment form, deposit the amount, and submit your documents to apply.
Which Monthly Income Plans provide a guaranteed monthly income?
Some monthly income plans offering guaranteed monthly income include POMIS, non-cumulative FDs, etc.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
full_pdf_text:
Monthly Income Scheme
In the world of investments, when it comes to payouts, some investments offer a lump sum amount, while others have the provision of offering monthly income. If you want a regular source of income, you can consider avenues that offer the flexibility of offering monthly payouts. There are different types of schemes offering a monthly income benefit. Some offer assured returns, while some are market-linked. Let’s understand the meaning of monthly income schemes and which investment avenues fit the bill.
Understanding Monthly Income Schemes
In simple words, monthly income schemes depict investments that can offer monthly payouts. In other words, you can get a regular source of income from these avenues. This income can help in supplementing your primary source of income or become your source of income after retirement.
There are different types of monthly investment plans available in the market. Each scheme has its own risk profile, investment tenure, tax implications, and the type of return that it offers.
Features of Monthly Income Plans
Some of the salient features of monthly income plans are as follows -
1. Monthly Payout Options
As is evident from the name itself, these types of plans offer monthly payouts. You can either get the interest earned on your investment as monthly income or withdraw a part of your investment every month. 2. Investment Flexibility
Many monthly income plans allow you to invest affordably. You can invest online or offline, making the scheme flexible and easily accessible.
3. Payout Choices
While monthly income plans offer monthly income, in some plans, you have the choice to customise the payout frequency. For instance, non-cumulative fixed deposits allow you to choose to receive the income every quarter, half-year, or year, rather than monthly. Given this scope of customisation, you can choose a payout option that matches your financial needs.
Monthly Income Scheme Options
Some of the common investment options offering monthly income payouts are as follows -
1. Post Office Monthly Income Scheme (POMIS)1
The Post Office Monthly Income Scheme is a low-risk, fixed-income investment scheme. You can make a lump sum deposit, and the tenure of the scheme is 5 years. The interest rate is 7.40% per annum, which is paid monthly. The benefits of POMIS include the following -
o Low Minimum Investment
You can start investing in the POMIS with as low as ₹1000 and in multiples of ₹1000. The maximum deposit amount is ₹9 lakhs for a single account and ₹15 lakhs for joint accounts. o Flexible Tenure
While the tenure of the account is 5 years, you are allowed to withdraw your deposit after one year from the date on which the account was opened. If you close the account before the completion of 3 years, 2% of the deposited amount would be deducted. If you close the account after completing 3 years but before maturity, 1% of the deposited amount would be deducted. 2. Fixed Deposits (FDs)
Fixed deposits (FDs) are also considered low-risk investment options as they offer fixed returns on your investments2. You can deposit a lump sum amount for a specific term and get a guaranteed interest rate2. Fixed deposits are offered by both banks and financial institutions, and 5-year FDs offered by banks are also allowed as a deduction under Section 80C2. The benefits of FDs are as follows -
o Fixed Interest Rates2
The interest rate of the FD is fixed when you open the account. Once fixed, it does not change over the investment tenure. You can enjoy fixed returns without market volatility and build a guaranteed corpus. o Options for Maturity2
On maturity, you have the option to reinvest your FD for continued investment. Alternatively, you can withdraw the FD and use the proceeds to fund your financial needs. o Interest Payment Options2
If you choose cumulative FDs, the interest income gets accumulated with the deposited amount. The total interest earned and the deposited amount are, then, paid on maturity. However, with non-cumulative FDs, you can choose to receive the interest income monthly, half-yearly, annually, or quarterly. On maturity, the principal amount is paid back. o Security3
FDs issued by banks are insured up to ₹5 lakhs by the DICGC (Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation). This minimises the credit risk of the deposit and secures your investment. 3. National Pension Scheme (NPS)
The National Pension System (NPS) is a retirement-oriented saving scheme which enjoys government backing. The scheme helps you invest in market-linked securities and earn market-linked returns over time. Some of the benefits of the NPS scheme are as follows -
o Investment Alternatives
NPS offers you a choice of two types of accounts – Tier I and Tier II. While Tier I is mandatory, Tier II is optional4. Moreover, you get a choice of two types of investment strategies – Auto Choice and Active Choice5. In Auto Choice the contributions are invested in a Life-cycle fund in which the proportion of funds invested across three asset classes are determined by a pre-defined portfolio and would change as per your age 5. However, under the Active strategy, you can choose how your contribution is to be invested in the permitted asset classes5. There are different types of asset classes under NPS, like equity, corporate debt, government securities, etc. You can choose an asset class that matches your risk appetite and investment preference5. o Portability
If your employer is also contributing to the NPS scheme, you don’t have to worry about continuity even when you change jobs. The NPS account is completely portable across all employers, even in different geographical locations6. o Retirement Benefits 8
Normal Exit – For Government subscribers: Normal exit occurs on superannuation/retirement as per applicable service rules, including employer-certified cases of invalidation, disability or specified premature retirements under the CCS (Implementation of NPS) Rules, 2021. On exit, if the accumulated corpus is less than ₹8 lakh, up to 100% may be withdrawn as a lump sum; for an accumulated corpus of ₹8–12 lakh, up to ₹6 lakh may be withdrawn with the balance through annuity or structured withdrawals; for corpus greater than ₹12 lakh, up to 60% may be withdrawn as a lump sum, with at least 40% used to purchase an annuity. Deferment of lump sum and/or annuity is permitted, with the ability to remain invested up to age 85.
Normal Exit - For Non-government subscribers: If the accumulated corpus is less than ₹8 lakh, up to 100% may be withdrawn as a lump sum; for corpus between ₹8–12 lakh, up to ₹6 lakh may be withdrawn with the balance through annuity or structured withdrawals; for corpus less than ₹12 lakh, at least 20% is to be used for annuity purchase and up to 80% may be withdrawn as a lump sum. Deferment of lump sum and/or annuity is permitted, with the ability to remain invested up to age 85.
This withdrawn corpus can be used for meeting your expenses and financial goals after retirement. Moreover, the remaining corpus is used to pay annuities, which can become a retirement benefit and ensure a source of regular income in your golden years. 4. National Savings Certificate (NSC)7
The National Savings Certificate is another investment scheme that offers fixed returns. It is available through post offices and authorised banks, and you can open an account with as little as ₹1000. The current interest rate for NSC is 7.7% per annum for the January to March quarter of financial year 2025-26.
o Safety
The scheme is backed by the Indian government. The interest rates are fixed, and you get the deposited amount plus the interest earned on maturity. o Maturity Period
The NSC has a tenure of 5 years after which it matures. Though partial withdrawals are allowed, they might reduce your overall earnings.
Factors to Consider Before Choosing a Monthly Income Plan
With the range of monthly income plans available, you get the choice of choosing a plan that fits your financial needs. However, before choosing, it is important to assess the following factors –
1. Risk Tolerance
Assess your risk appetite, i.e., your risk-taking ability. This would help you choose the right investment plan. For instance, individuals with high risk appetite consider NPS as a suitable option as it gives market-linked returns. On the other hand, if you don’t like risks and want assured returns, FDs or POMIS can be a more suitable fit. 2. Liquidity
Liquidity means how soon you can convert your investments into cash. Assess your liquidity needs to choose the correct avenues. If you need liquidity, you might choose investment options that do not have a premature withdrawal penalty that might hamper your returns. On the other hand, if liquidity is not a concern, you can choose avenues with a lock-in period. 3. Investment Horizon
The investment horizon means how long you can stay invested. Different investment avenues have different investment tenures. So, it is recommended to choose avenues whose tenures match your investment horizon. 4. Minimum Investment
Check the minimum investment required for the investment that you prefer. Ensure that your savings meet the minimum investment criteria so that you can successfully invest in the scheme. 5. Tax Implications
Some monthly income schemes offer tax benefits on investments and, in some cases, also on the returns. Checking the tax implications of each scheme can help you choose tax- efficient options that can help in reducing your tax liability.
6. Fees and Charges
Check the fees and charges associated with the different types of investment schemes so that you know the expenses of investing in the scheme. 7. Interest Rates
Knowing the interest rates of different schemes can help you compare and find a scheme that offers the maximum possible returns. 8. Diversification
Diversification means choosing different types of assets in your portfolio so that you can mitigate investment risks and enhance the return potential. So, try to diversify your investments across equity, debt, and other asset classes. 9. Professional Advice
If you are new to investments or if you need expert assistance in selecting the right monthly income schemes, you can seek professional advice.
Who Should Choose a Monthly Income Plan?
A monthly income plan might be suitable for the following types of individuals -
1. Retirees and Pensioners
Individuals who have retired and earn a pension might choose monthly income plans to create a source of regular income for meeting their lifestyle expenses. 2. Risk-Averse Investors
If you are averse to investment risks, you can choose fixed-income monthly income plans. These plans would offer guaranteed monthly which would not be affected due to market volatility. 3. Individuals with Irregular Income
If your income is not fixed, monthly income plans can help create a regular source of income. This can help you meet your everyday expenses without straining your budget. 4. People Planning for Short to Medium Term Goals
If you have to save up for a short or medium-term goal, a monthly income plan can prove useful. With fixed-income plans, you can create a secure corpus and enjoy capital safety. 5. Senior Citizens Seeking Tax Benefits
As many monthly income plans offer tax-saving benefits, senior citizens can not only create a regular income stream but also save taxes on the same with such plans.
Key Takeaways
1. Monthly income plans are investment schemes that have the potential to generate monthly
income inflows.
2. The features of monthly income plans include regular interest payouts, flexibility in
investment, and customisable payout options. 3. Some of the common monthly income schemes include POMIS, National Pension Scheme,
FDs, and NSC. 4. When selecting a monthly income plan, consider your risk appetite, investment duration, tax
implications, minimum investment, interest offered, and other factors. 5. Diversify your portfolio across asset classes to minimise risks and enhance the return
potential. 6. Monthly income plans are suitable for senior citizens, retirees and pensioners, investors
having a low risk profile, investors saving for short or medium term goals, and individuals with irregular incomes. Conclusion
Different types of monthly income schemes have different risk profiles, return potential, tax implications, and investment tenures. Understand each scheme thoroughly before investing in it. Choose a scheme if it matches your financial goals, investment period, and risk appetite. Also, try to diversify and seek professional help if needed.
FAQs
1. Which is the Right Monthly Income Scheme (MIS)?
The right monthly income scheme depends on your financial goals, risk-taking ability, investment period, and affordability. You can compare the available options and choose a scheme that matches your financial needs. 2. What are the key benefits of investing in a Monthly Income Scheme?
Some of the key benefits of investing in monthly income schemes include a regular source of inflow, customised payout options, tax savings with some instruments. 3. Which Monthly Income Plans are considered low-risk?
Plans that offer fixed and guaranteed returns can be considered low-risk since they are not exposed to market volatility. Moreover, if the plans are backed by the government, they do not have any credit risk, making them safer. 4. How can one apply for a Monthly Income Scheme?
You can apply for a monthly income scheme through the issuer. Choose the scheme, fill out the investment form, deposit the amount, and submit your documents to apply. 5. Which Monthly Income Plans provide a guaranteed monthly income?
Some monthly income plans offering guaranteed monthly income include POMIS, non- cumulative FDs, etc.

changes.word_original: However, if the corpus is up to ₹8 lakhs, you can withdraw the entire amount in a lump sum.
changes.pdf_final: Normal Exit - For Non-government subscribers: If the accumulated corpus is less than ₹8 lakh, up to 100% may be withdrawn as a lump sum; for corpus between ₹8–12 lakh, up to ₹6 lakh may be withdrawn with the balance through annuity or structured withdrawals; for corpus less than ₹12 lakh, at least 20% is to be used for annuity purchase and up to 80% may be withdrawn as a lump sum.
Deferment of lump sum and/or annuity is permitted, with the ability to remain invested up to age 85.

changes.word_original: Moreover, the remaining corpus is used to pay annuities, which can become a retirement benefit and ensure a source of regular income in your golden years.
changes.pdf_final: Moreover, the remaining corpus is used to pay annuities, which can become a retirement benefit and ensure a source of regular income in your golden years. 4.

changes.word_original: The current interest rate for NSC is 7.7% per annum.
changes.pdf_final: The current interest rate for NSC is 7.7% per annum for the January to March quarter of financial year 2025-26.
